pattern: Because diagonal moves cover both axes for free, the fastest possible arrival time is the larger of the two coordinate distances, and any extra time beyond that can always be burned by wiggling back and forth -- except needing exactly one leftover second, which never works.
hints: With 8-directional moves, one step can change both coordinates by 1 at once, so the fewest seconds needed to get from start to finish is the Chebyshev distance: the larger of the row gap and the column gap.
If you have more seconds than that minimum, you can always spend the extra ones by stepping away and back (two seconds cancel out), so any total at or above the minimum works -- with one exception.

If start and finish are already the same cell, zero seconds trivially works, but exactly one second never does (you must move somewhere and cannot return in a single step) -- every other second count, including zero, is fine.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, reachable with time to spare
same start and finish with zero seconds
same start and finish with exactly one second, impossible
same start and finish with two seconds, possible by wiggling
diagonal move at the minimum distance exactly
coordinates near the maximum value with a tiny displacement
row distance and column distance differ, minimum time is the larger one
one second short of the minimum required

stress.description: random small displacements with a random extra time budget, checked against a BFS layer simulation
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    sx = rng.randint(1, 50)
    sy = rng.randint(1, 50)
    fx = rng.randint(1, 50)
    fy = rng.randint(1, 50)
    dist = max(abs(sx - fx), abs(sy - fy))
    extra = rng.randint(0, 10)
    t = dist + extra
    return [sx, sy, fx, fy, t]
